options.text: Server-side rendering for better performance and SEO

ShortQuestions.question: Explain the difference between a React SPA and Next.js. What problems does Next.js solve?
ShortQuestions.preferredAnswer:
**React SPA (Single-Page Application):**

Flow:
```
User visits → Browser gets HTML (nearly empty) → Downloads JS bundle → JS executes → React renders → Content shows
```

Characteristics:
- ❌ Slow initial load (must download and execute JS first)
- ❌ Poor SEO (search engines see empty HTML)
- ❌ Client-side rendering only
- ❌ Manual routing setup (React Router)
- ❌ No built-in API routes (need separate backend)
- ❌ Manual optimization (code splitting, images, etc.)

**Next.js:**

Flow:
```
User visits → Server renders React to HTML → Browser gets full HTML → Content shows instantly → JS downloads → Hydration for interactivity
```

Characteristics:
- ✅ Fast initial load (HTML ready immediately)
- ✅ Excellent SEO (search engines see full content)
- ✅ Server-side + client-side rendering
- ✅ File-based routing (automatic)
- ✅ Built-in API routes
- ✅ Automatic optimization (code splitting, images, fonts)

**Problems Next.js solves:**

1. **Performance** — Server renders HTML, users see content faster
2. **SEO** — Search engines see full content immediately
3. **Developer Experience** — No configuration needed, everything built-in
4. **Full-stack** — Backend API routes in the same project
5. **Optimization** — Image optimization, code splitting, font optimization automatic

**When to use each:**
- **React SPA**: Internal dashboards, admin panels (SEO doesn't matter)
- **Next.js**: Public websites, e-commerce, blogs (SEO critical)

ShortQuestions.question: Explain file-based routing in Next.js App Router. How do you create: a static route, a dynamic route, and a catch-all route?
What are layouts in Next.js? Create a root layout with a header and footer, and a nested layout for a blog section.
ShortQuestions.preferredAnswer:
**Layouts** are UI components that wrap pages and persist across navigations. They're perfect for shared elements like headers, footers, and sidebars. **Root Layout (Required)** ```tsx // app/layout.tsx export const metadata = { title: 'My Next.js App', description: 'A modern web application', }; export default function RootLayout({ children }) { return ( {/* Header - shows on all pages */}

My App

Home About Blog Contact
{/* Main content - page.tsx renders here */}
{children}
{/* Footer - shows on all pages */}

© 2024 My App. All rights reserved.

Privacy Terms
); } ``` **Nested Layout for Blog** ``` app/ ├── layout.tsx (Root layout) ├── page.tsx (Home) ├── about/ │ └── page.tsx (About) └── blog/ ├── layout.tsx (Blog layout - nested) ├── page.tsx (Blog list) └── [id]/ └── page.tsx (Blog post) ``` ```tsx // app/blog/layout.tsx export default function BlogLayout({ children }) { return (
{/* Blog sidebar */}

ShortQuestions.question: Explain the difference between Server Components and Client Components. When should you use each?
ShortQuestions.preferredAnswer:
**Server Components** (default in App Router): ```tsx // No 'use client' = Server Component export default async function UsersPage() { // ✅ Can fetch data with async/await const users = await fetch('https://api.example.com/users') .then(res => res.json()); return (
    {users.map(user =>
  • {user.name}
    • )}
); } ``` **Can do:** ✅ Async/await directly in component ✅ Access databases directly ✅ Read files from filesystem ✅ Use server-only libraries ✅ Keep sensitive data on server (API keys) ✅ Reduce client JavaScript bundle **Cannot do:** ❌ Use React hooks (useState, useEffect, etc.) ❌ Use browser APIs (window, localStorage, etc.) ❌ Add event handlers (onClick, onChange, etc.) ❌ Use browser-only libraries **Client Components** (add 'use client'): ```tsx 'use client'; // ← Makes this a Client Component import { useState } from 'react'; export default function Counter() { // ✅ Can use useState const [count, setCount] = useState(0); // ✅ Can use event handlers return (

Count: {count}

); } ``` **Can do:** ✅ Use React hooks (useState, useEffect, etc.) ✅ Add event handlers (onClick, onChange, etc.) ✅ Use browser APIs (window, localStorage, etc.) ✅ Use browser-only libraries ✅ Add interactivity **Cannot do:** ❌ Use async component syntax ❌ Directly access databases ❌ Access server-only resources **Decision Tree:** ``` Does it need interactivity? ├─ Yes → Client Component └─ No → Server Component Does it use React hooks? ├─ Yes → Client Component └─ No → Server Component Does it fetch data? ├─ On server (async) → Server Component └─ On client (useEffect) → Client Component Does it use browser APIs? ├─ Yes → Client Component └─ No → Server Component ``` **Best Practice: Combine Both** ```tsx // app/page.tsx (Server Component) import Counter from './components/Counter'; // Client Component async function getData() { const res = await fetch('https://api.example.com/data'); return res.json(); } export default async function HomePage() { const data = await getData(); // Server-side data fetching return (

Server-rendered: {data.title}

{/* Client-side interactivity */}
); } ``` **When to use Server Components:** - Fetching data from APIs/databases - Rendering static content - Accessing backend resources - Keeping sensitive data secure - Reducing JavaScript bundle size **When to use Client Components:** - Forms and inputs - Buttons with onClick - Modals, dropdowns, tooltips - Animations - Browser APIs (localStorage, geolocation) - Third-party libraries that need browser **Rule:** Use Server Components by default. Only add 'use client' when you need interactivity or browser features.
